Transcrição Completa: 72 When to raise your pricing and how 10 5 24

Brandon specifically asked about how do you know and what do you do to figure out when it's a good time to raise your pricing and uh I do this all the time and I encourage a lot of you guys to do this all the time."
[00:20] "So rather than isolating it to individual conversations, I figured we can bring it into a collective weekly group call and address it amongst all of us. [00:38] "Yes."
[00:39] "Okay, great. What do you charge right now?"

[01:31] "So when you say at the end, you mean there's a timetable to the 10K. How long's the timetable?"
[01:36] "Six months."
[01:37] "So within six months, somebody doubles their revenue paying 10K. What's the average person's revenue that they double?"

[04:52] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Revenue Growth Calculation"
NÚMEROS: $10K → $20K monthly, $60K total increase over 6 months, 6% fee vs value delivered
VISUAL: Cálculo matemático mostrando disparidade entre preço cobrado ($10K) e valor entregue ($60K), demonstração visual de ROI de 6x
[04:53] "So, to be clear, within six months, you're going to net them or gross them $60,000 on average extra."
[05:01] "Yeah."
[05:02] "And you're only charging 10K."
[05:04] "Yeah."
[05:05] "So, what's what's 10K divided by 60? 6%."
[05:10] "Yeah."
[05:11] "I'm sorry, that's the opposite."
[05:13] "Yeah."
[05:14] "Yeah. 6%. So, to be clear, 125 like what? Why such a small bump when there's such a significant difference? That's a six times difference in return that they generate within that six months. And then obviously that revenue sticks with them forever. Right."
[05:30] "Yeah."
[05:31] "Right. So to be clear, the upfront price is the friction point. Okay. That will get more people to say yes or more people to say no. What does pricing also signal?"
[05:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Price Signaling: A5 Kobe Beef Example"
NÚMEROS: $120 per ounce vs discount store prices, 10x+ price difference
VISUAL: Comparação visual entre carne premium (A5 Kobe) e carne comum, demonstração de como preço sinaliza qualidade, mesmo sendo produto similar
[05:44] "So like I'll give you I'll give you a different example. Okay. Uh, how many people here put in the chat have had A5 kobe beef straight from Japan? Put in the chat yes or no if you've had A5 kobe beef. I'm not talking about the gimmick of Wagyu. I'm talking about kobe, the pure stuff."
[06:04] "Okay. Yes. Yes. Yes. Once again, put in the chat if you've had A5 Kobe beef. Only Wagyu. Sorry. Sorry, Griffin. You're missing out. Honestly, they're pretty similar. Fucking same thing. I'm vegetarian."
[06:20] "Okay, so check this out. The difference in cost. Okay, between like a regular let's talk let's talk prime steak. Okay, so you have like choice prime I don't even know what's below choice on choice prime wagyu kobe beef you know somewhere in between there's like dry age steaks have the same things."
[06:37] "My point being the difference there. Really, the only difference is the marbling and the branding and like the impression of what the steak is. Okay, sure there's like a slight difference in in flavor based on the fat content, but like at the end of the day, it's really just branding and like what it's become psychologically to people in order to pay more."
[06:57] "Okay, but at the end of the day, like if I go I go so tomorrow's my girl's birthday. I'm going to go to Nobu tomorrow for dinner and Nobu will charge upwards of like $120 an ounce for fucking A5 kobe beef on a little hot stone that they catch on fire in front of you."
[07:14] "You know why would I pay $120 an ounce? Well, to be clear, what's the price signal about that steak? About that cut of steak versus, okay, if I go to fucking Walmart or Dollar General and I buy a ribeye, okay, and it's cheaper, what does that signal about that steak in terms of its price being heavily discounted? What's it signal?"
[07:36] "Low quality versus high quality"
[07:38] "100%. It signals even though it's the same thing, it's totally different, you know? It signals, well, there's got to be a difference when it's fucking $120 an ounce in comparison to like, dude, for $120 in some parts of the United States, I could probably buy a quarter of a cow."
[07:55] "You know what I mean? So, like, think about that difference quality, right? So, the signal of, okay, you charge 10K. Well, it's like what what's the signal there versus you charge 125, like is there really that big of a difference in what you're going to signal between those two price points?"
[08:13] "No. Right? So to be clear, let me give you let me give you an example. Okay? If I want to address what I signal in my pricing, I want to address what other people are charging for the same things. And I want to consider just from my perspective on them. How do I view that person?"
[08:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Pricing Strategy: Agency Example"
NÚMEROS: $15K/month + 10% net rev share vs $5K/month + 0% rev share
VISUAL: Comparação lado a lado de estruturas de preço, demonstração de incentivos alinhados, visualização de potencial de ganhos ($100K wire transfer goal)
[08:32] "How do I view what that individual is selling? How do I view what that company's product is going to do for people? You know, how do I interpret the quality of what they're selling? How do I qual how do I interpret like the system that they're selling people, you know, in order to get the results that they get?"
[08:49] "It all starts with just a simple understanding of the signal that we give off when we when we price. Okay. So, for starters, sometimes it's not even about the difference in result and how fucked you're getting in comparison to what you provide. Sometimes it's just as simple as do I want to signal something better in comparison to what others are signaling."
[09:09] "I'm a marketing agency. I charge 15K and 10% 15K a month, 10% net rev share. There's people in this very group that I teach as marketing agencies that charge less than me, but quite literally learn from me and do the same things as me, but they charge like 5k a month. And like some of them don't even charge rev shares."
[09:29] "You know what's the difference in what they signal versus what I signal? Just based on price, not based on value, just based on price. What's the signal there? Well, obviously I have a high probability making a fuckload more money when I'm charging that amount, don't I?"
[09:44] "Okay, now watch this. Incentives. Okay, talk about the incentive of it. So price and incentives go hand in hand. Let's use the same example I just provided. 15K a month, 10% versus 5K and no rev share. What's that pricing in? What's that pricing signal as an incentive?"
[10:03] "Somebody said anybody. Nobody knows."
[10:06] "You're incentivized to get results for your clients because you're charging the rev share. Is that what you're asking?"
[10:12] "It's definitely one incentive for sure. What else? None of you think of incentives when you price."
[10:18] "I mean, the client has to move their ass to like make the money too with you, right? So, they have to collaborate more."
[10:24] "That's definitely a good incentive. Yes, I absolutely attempt to signal that in my pricing for sure. That's that's another component of it. Absolutely. Anybody else?"
[10:33] "Incentive to be profitable instead of just juice rev"
[10:36] "100%. Yep. Look, I signal an incentive with that price of I'm here to make you a fuckload of money and I want to make a fuckload of money, too, because I have a percentage of the net rev share."
[10:48] "I'm also signaling because I do it on a net rev share that I need you to net a lot of money in order for me to make a lot of money in the relationship. I also tell people when they ask me my pricing, I say straight up, I want you to wire me 100K in a month. That's my goal in this relationship."
[11:06] "What's that mean? Well, that means they got to net a million. The guy charging 5K a month, no rev share, what's his what's his incentive that he's signaling? Make you back enough to continue getting paid 5K."
[11:20] "See the difference?"
[11:22] "Yeah."
[11:23] "Are are those things typically spoken when you look at a price or are they subconsciously understood? You ever go to a burger spot and it's like fucking $50 for a burger? What does that signal right away? Do you talk to him about that? Do you say, 'Hm, you got a $50 burger over here. That must be really good.' No, you just fucking assume that it's going to be the shit right?"
[11:44] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Subconscious Price Assumptions"
NÚMEROS: $50 burger example
VISUAL: Lista de suposições automáticas: melhor qualidade de carne, vegetais frescos, pães artesanais, cozinha mais limpa, equipe mais bem paga, clientela premium
[11:46] "It has to be. It's $50. You know what I mean? So, to be clear, I also assume the quality is going to be different, right? I assume that the quality of that burger is going to be better. I assume that I assume the beef's going to be better. I assume the vegetables are going to be greater. I assume their buns are going to be homemade."
[12:03] "I assume like the kitchen's going to be cleaner. I assume the staff are going to be friendlier to me because they're probably more well paid. You know, I expect better people in the restaurant in addition to all that, don't I?"
[12:15] "You see what I'm saying? Pricing once again signals the quality. And in addition to that, pricing has an incentive structure aligned with it as well in terms of what we give off just based on our pricing alone."
[12:27] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Friction Test: Inner Circle Example"
NÚMEROS: 16 people joined in September
VISUAL: Demonstração de ausência de fricção como indicador de preço baixo, conceito de "value ceiling"
[12:29] "And the initial thing that I spoke about just to be clear is okay, when we look at the fact that we could raise our prices, the simple question is do we get any kind of friction when we tell people what our cost is? There were like 16 of you that joined into the inner circle in September. What did that tell me about my pricing?"
[12:48] "too low."
[12:50] "Literally, question for all of you. Did any of you actually have any friction when you bought this? Be honest if you did."
[12:58] "It's my first mastermind ever. So, just yeah, a little bit. But after one month, it there's nothing."
[13:06] "So my point being first of all this is in this particular offers case this is like an actually limited offer okay it's not like fake shit like we have I promise everybody come to the Q4 mastermind you'll see for yourself you'll be like yeah we needed to slow this down okay"
[13:22] "when you come to the penthouse in Q1 same thing you'll be like okay I appreciate the fact that there's not infinite fucking people in this room okay so for us it's like we we'll continue raising the price until we literally start getting friction and people start saying like h you know I don't know that's too because we're intentionally attempting to slow it down."
[13:43] "But my point is, okay, in most offers that's not the case. Like for most of you guys, you would not attempt to slow down the quantity of people that are signing up. But my point being the lesson there is, is there friction or not?"
[13:56] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Hummus Guy Example: Zero Price Resistance"
NÚMEROS: $230 purchase, zero price questions asked
VISUAL: Demonstração de compra sem fricção, indicador de oportunidade de aumento de preço
[13:58] "Sometimes people buy stuff and there's just it's like a no-brainer. It's just, oh yeah, I could buy so much more of that. I went to this hummus guy the other day. I didn't I didn't even ask a single fucking thing about the price. I bought and I ended up buying $230 of hummus."
[14:14] "You know, I have no idea what the guy charges for an individual hummus or all the random shit that he sold me with the hummus. I have no clue. Could he raise his pricing? Did I even ask about the pricing? What is that signal for him that he could do? There's no friction."
[14:30] "Okay. So, when you do have friction, it's important to note like you're probably close to a value ceiling. Okay. When there's no friction, you could raise the price immediately and you could likely benefit from still having no friction or finding that you're now at the value ceiling."
[14:46] "The value ceiling being okay when friction starts to occur in a process where people are considering it based on price alone. That means that what they're buying in exchange for that money, it either needs to be much more articulate and clear for why the value exchange is equal or greater in their favor."
[15:04] "And sometimes you have to articulate things better or sometimes you have to tweak the offers when you start getting to that point of friction. Okay? But to be clear, in most instances there's a lot of people who just criminally undercharge themselves."
[15:18] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "80% Close Rate Problem"
NÚMEROS: 80% close rate = criminal undercharging
VISUAL: Demonstração de que alta taxa de conversão indica preço muito baixo, não habilidade de vendas
[15:20] "They have no friction in their sales process. I don't remember who it was. Somebody in the inner circle the other day was bragging about having an 80% close rate. And I was like, why are you bragging about that? Like that just signals that you're criminally undercharging yourself."
[15:35] "Makes it makes no sense to close at 80%. Does that signal that you're a great closer or does it signal that you just have fucking lay down deals and that you're collecting far less in comparison to what you could?"
[15:47] "You see what I'm saying? So, it's like there's little things that are right in front of you right now that all tell you you could immediately charge more money."
[15:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Swan's 5-Day ROI Example"
NÚMEROS: 5 days to recover investment
VISUAL: Caso extremo de valor entregue vs preço cobrado, indicador claro de necessidade de aumento
[15:57] "To be clear and reiterate, are you getting fucked? Meaning once again, like take Swan in the inner circle. Swan just joined in within five days. within five days he made his money back. Okay, five days it's like could charge more. That's an example."
[16:15] "Okay, if I go out there and I look at what my price is signaling to people, okay, am I signaling low quality? Am I signaling high quality? Sometimes you could raise it just based on that basis alone."
[16:27] "Two, the incentives. What's my pricing signaling as an incentive? Okay, is it signaling I'm actually going to give a shit and be here in the long haul and help people? you know, is it signaling that it's too low and that it's I'm I'm not going to give a shit about the other party."
[16:44] "You know, what's a signal to be clear for friction? Okay, if there's no friction, I know right away I can charge more money. Okay, if I do have friction, then I know I potentially need to add additional value to the offer or I'm potentially needing to articulate the value in the offer differently and more clearly. Okay?"
[17:04] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Ed the Tax Strategist Case Study"
NÚMEROS: $12,500 fee to save $300K-$400K annually, 10% of savings structure proposed
VISUAL: Exemplo de precificação que soa "too good to be true", reestruturação de incentivos com fee upfront + percentual de economia
[17:06] "And then once again, six. So, I'm going to give you another example. I'm on the phone yesterday with a inner circle member named Ed. Ed's a CPA. He's he's actually a tax strategist. Does anybody here know what a tax strategist is? What is it?"
[17:22] "They help you with just um like different ways to either like reduce your tax liability or um just kind of start preparing for the end of the year and stuff like that too. But if you're over earning or if you're going to be paying a large tax bill, it's finding other opportunities to either like house that money somewhere else or invest it somewhere else."
[17:41] "All with the intention to lower your tax burden."
[17:44] "Yeah."
[17:45] "And in addition to that, increase asset retention and potentially cash flow, too. To be clear, Ed has a two call close and he wants to reduce it to a one call close. So I asked him, I'm like, 'Roll play with me real quick, Ed. Like, talk me through what you do on a sales call.'"
[18:02] "And long story short, we get to the end. Anybody want to guess what Ed charges? He told me, listen to this scenario real quick and then take a guess. Okay. He says, 'Jeremy, based on your situation, you know, I think without exaggeration, I think you could save like 3 to 400k a year in your tax burden'"
[18:23] "in addition to that. Um, we could help you acquire assets at the same time. So the money just doesn't get spent on random stuff. You'd actually be acquiring assets in addition to reducing your taxes by that substantial amount. And I think we can cash flow a little bit too. You know, probably a couple percentage points a year based on that investment amount."
[18:42] "And I'm like, 'All right, cool. What's it cost, Ed? What do you think he said? What do you think his price was to save me 3 to 400K a year? Take a guess."
[18:52] "10K. Who else?"
[18:55] "6K."
[18:56] "6K. 5K."
[18:59] "So Ed Ed said 12,500. What's he What's he signal when he says that? Like, does that sound fair? I believe I to be clear, I believe Ed's going to be able to do that. But my point is is like what what's it give off when he says that?"
[19:18] "Scarcity mentality as well."
[19:20] "No, say it again."
[19:22] "Scarcity mentality. Maybe he doesn't have that much deal. He really wants the deal. So he doesn't want to charge you too much either. That's sometimes what I felt when I when I end up charge."
[19:32] "You know, when he said that, I thought to myself, I thought, dude, this sounds too good to be true now. You know, I thought to myself, I was like, I don't know, man. I mean, he going to save me that much. He's only going to capture 125. I don't know, man. It doesn't make much sense to me."
[19:51] "And I asked him, I'm like, 'Ed, have you ever tried to charge more?' He goes, 'No.' I'm like, 'Why do you only charge 125?' He was like, 'I don't know. I mean, I'm not that great at sales.'"
[20:03] "And uh yeah, I was like, 'Well, I mean, you know, not terrible at sales, but I mean, you could use some framing differences at the beginning of the call. That would probably help.' I started tweaking his sales process a little bit to help him just with the one call close."
[20:17] "But, you know, my point being, it's like, dude, that 125 price point, it almost sounded like a scam. You know, it almost sounded like like how how is he going to save me that much and only charge that little? You know, didn't make much sense. The incentive wasn't clear."
[20:34] "I would have actually been more sold, believe it or not, if he would have said, 'Okay, so we charge 125 upfront cuz I got I got some payroll positions that I got to pay and we actually will take a percentage of what we save you.'"
[20:48] "So, as an example, like we'll take 10% of what we save you. So, if I save you like 300 grand, you're going to you're going to pay me 125 upfront and you're going to pay me 30 grand if I can save you 300."
[21:01] "I would be like, 'That makes a lot more sense.' That also seems like you have a much better incentive to actually give a shit about saving me more money. It would signal that you likely know what you're doing as well because you're confident enough to charge that."
[21:15] "And in addition to that, it feels like there still wouldn't be friction because he's still getting his 125 upfront, but in addition to that, he's getting more money on the back end. You know, it feels like it would align like perfectly to the expectations of what I'd want out of a tax strategist if I hired somebody like that."
[21:33] "You see what I'm saying? You see the alignment there? That's 6. When everything aligns with the pricing and all these things are all checked, it just comes to this conclusion mentally for the buyer of like, dude, this is the shit this is my guy right here. You know, this makes perfect sense."
[21:50] "It almost removes a lot of objections when all those things align. When one of them's off, you get a little bit of friction. Does that make sense?"
[21:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Key Action Item: Double Your Price Test"
NÚMEROS: 2x current price, test with few calls only
VISUAL: Estratégia de teste de preço, conceito de "just a test", reversibilidade da decisão, descoberta do value ceiling
[22:00] "So, here's my point just to reiterate a few key things. Number one, if you get no friction, the very first thing I want you to do is literally just double whatever you're charging right now. I want you to see if you start getting friction with it. Okay?"
[22:15] "As crazy as that sounds, there's, you know, this like bias inside of most of our heads that, you know, you just been doing things for a certain way for so long. And you likely think to yourself like, what the stability comes from things being relatively the same."
[22:30] "You know, there's so much chaos in the world when we introduce significant change to our own lives. You know, it's like it burdens us mentally just based on the change aspect of it. But to be clear, I I just want to sell you on the fact real quick. All you're doing is a little test. It's just a little test. That's all it is."
[22:48] "You're just going to see with a few people. Doesn't have to be everybody. Doesn't have to be a full month. Just a few sales calls. Just double your price and just see what people say. You can always go back. You can give them a deal and charge what you previously charged if there's actual resistance and friction you experience."
[23:05] "But what's the worst that can happen there? Somebody somebody try to argue with me. What's the worst that can happen if you tried to do that? Is there an argument for it?"
[23:14] "A couple people say no. You get feedback and then you you you you pivot."
[23:19] "You found the you found the ceiling. You found the value ceiling, right? which in itself uncovers a whole different problem, right? Good thing, not a bad thing."
[23:28] "To be clear, that's one of the easiest things you could do to make more money. It's one of the easiest levers to pull. Just charge more money. And don't do like, listen, remember what Mason said? I was like, 'Well, how much more could you charge?' What did he say? He said he can go from 10k to 125."
[23:46] "Like, what the fuck that going to do for him? That means every four deals he makes an extra 10 grand. The fuck Mason going to do with an extra 10 grand in a month? Go to dinner. You know, like for real, what's the impact of 10 grand in a month for Mason?"
[24:02] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Mason's Math: Real Impact"
NÚMEROS: $10K → $20K = $40K extra on 4 deals vs $10K → $12.5K = $10K extra on 4 deals
VISUAL: Comparação matemática de impacto real, demonstração de que pequenos aumentos não criam mudança significativa
[24:04] "You see what I'm saying? Versus if he goes from 10K to 20K, well, those extra four deals get him a $40,000 difference. You know, that's money. And that's on four deals. And he said half his deals are on payment plans anyway. See what I'm saying? It's not that tough."
[24:22] "Some of you are so valuable to the buyers that you have. When's the last time, Simple question, put in the chat, when's the last time you tried to increase your pricing? How long's it been?"
[24:33] "January, five months ago. What happened in January? What were you charging? What did you start charging?"
[24:40] "Six months."
[24:41] "I was charging 5K. No, I I had just gone up to 7,800."
[24:46] "I tested 9K and my close rate dropped in half."
[24:49] "So, you found the ceiling."
[24:51] "Yeah. So, I went back down."
[24:53] "Smart. Good."
[24:54] "30 days. Brandon, what were you charging versus what what are you charging now?"
[24:59] "So, we were charging uh9.97 for the product and then we increased it to uh $12.97. So, it was a 30% increase."
[25:10] "Any difference in stats?"
[25:12] "Uh, no. We actually did really well. We we we uh we actually timed it with a relaunch of the product. So, we did 350K that month we crushed. Um, and then no, I mean the generally it's kind of held steady, so no real friction here at this point."
[25:28] "Nice. Good. Okay, cool."
[25:31] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Price Increase Communication Strategy"
NÚMEROS: $2K → $3K → $4K monthly progression example
VISUAL: Estratégia de comunicação: timeline claro, anúncio formal, limpeza de pipeline, conceito de anchor bias
[25:33] "Adrian asked a good question in the chat. What's the best way to communicate to clients prices going up? So, Adrian, watch this. Okay, this is this is the the pros and cons to raising prices. Okay, number one, pros obviously make more money. Okay, cons, you're going to clear out the pipeline. Okay."
[25:54] "So, whatever you currently charge, you have to do a very clear like announcement style prices are going up. You have to be able to effectively communicate it to everybody in the pipeline. You have to be able to give a clear timeline to when it's going to happen."
[26:08] "Typically, it's not just an immediate thing unless nobody's in the pipeline. Okay? Or unless your pipeline's real time, meaning you have people that are literally just buying like by the day on some kind of digital product."
[26:20] "In most instances, in almost all instances, you have a pipeline. When I say you clear it out, so as an example, we use the inner circle, right? 2K a month, 3K a month. It'll go from 3K a month to like I'll just fucking literally keep lifting it until we finally start getting people that say no."
[26:38] "Okay, to be clear, if I have the people at 3K a month and I raise it from 3K to 4K, what happens to the people who are all told it's 3K a month? Are they going to feel good about buying it at four?"
[26:50] "No. No. It's anchor bias. So those people, they have a really low probability to purchase because they knew that it could have been at one price. It almost feels like a punishment to them. You know, they didn't take action. Now they're being punished if they purchase it because now it's a higher price point in comparison to what it was. Okay?"
[27:10] "So, generally those people won't buy. They'll only buy if you give them a deal and charge them what the old price was. So, to be clear, you can typically see like a good lift in the sales volume in the short term as a result of increasing your pricing from clearing out the pipeline."
[27:26] "But once again, cons, you'll have a fresh pipeline right on the other side of that. the new people, they have no anchor bias against what the previous pricing was. So, at the end of the day, you shouldn't have any issues once you raise it."
[27:39] "But to be clear, you know, best way to communicate it is typical communication channels, Adrian, text, call, email, stories. Uh you can even do a post or two if you want. Um just make it known, you know, just make it known that way people don't feel unfair."
[27:55] "And uh main thing to be clear is typically the sales people in your case, Adrian, just given the price point of your product. Um, but yeah, long story short, look, it's a great thing. Great thing, not a bad thing. A lot more pros than cons in that scenario."
[28:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "COVID Shipping Container Example"
NÚMEROS: 4x increase in shipping costs during COVID
VISUAL: Exemplo de aumento de custo operacional, transferência de custo para consumidor, não absorção pela empresa
[28:12] "Okay, to be clear, there one more thing I want to talk about when it comes to raising pricing. Sometimes it's just sensible. Sometime you guys remember COVID, any of my e-commerce guys on the call, how much was a fucking shipping container during COVID?"
[28:26] "Dude, I remember I I had that big candle company. My shipping container costs were like four times greater to get shit from China to here. It was insane. Did I endure the burden of that cost? Did I shoulder that cost? Fuck no. I raised that cost and put it onto my consumer."
[28:43] "Okay, that's a sensible thing to do. When a supply chain related cost goes up on a business, the business doesn't take the profit hit. They hand it off to the customer. or the customer is one that that endures that burden. Okay."
[28:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Q4 Election Cycle Cost Inflation"
NÚMEROS: $25 → $60 per qualified lead (2.5x increase), costs spike on debate days
VISUAL: Flutuação de custos em Q4 eleitoral, estratégia de preço dinâmico diário/semanal
[29:00] "So to be clear, sometimes price is just once again it's like a it's like a real-time calculation. Meaning look, you can charge less or more relative to your hard costs that you have. So as an example, we're in Q4 right now officially as of 5 days ago."
[29:16] "We have an election cycle here in the United States this Q4. This is going to be one of the most fucked up Q4s ever. Okay, these happen every four years. It's insane the cost that can happen on a business."
[29:29] "Like I've seen, as an example, for key demographics in this case, this year specifically, it seems to be the wealthiest people that are getting the most inflation so far, like 5 days into Q4. Like I've seen costs go from like $25 a qualified lead for an accredited investor to like 60 overnight on a debate day or something like that."
[29:50] "Same thing happens. Will it temporarily drop back down in between key events? Yeah. Does that mean it's probable to fucking rock it up the closer that we get to the election? Yeah. What do you do in that scenario?"
[30:03] "Does the business burden themselves with that difference in it's like a fucking 2.5x difference in cost? You know, what do you do in those situations? Hey, raise the price. You raise the profit margin. You know, it's just a logical thing to do with that example."
[30:19] "Somebody's got to endure the cost. It's not going to be you. It's going to be the customer. Easier to do with hard goods. In addition to that, typically easier to do with digital goods as well. any kind any kind of service, any kind of product, it's just math."
[30:35] "Whatever the difference in CPA is in Q4 percentage-wise, that's going to be the difference in the price that the customer pays. It's a dynamic thing as well."
[30:44] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Dynamic Pricing Strategy in Q4"
NÚMEROS: Daily or weekly price adjustments based on real-time costs
VISUAL: Sistema de precificação dinâmica, ajustes diários baseados em CPA, comunicação com equipe de vendas
[30:46] "So, to be clear, the smartest people that we work with, no exaggeration, they have a dynamic price in Q4. Meaning, literally by the day or by the week, they're telling their salespeople, 'Okay, here's what today's price is based on based on yesterday's cost or based on today's cost so far.'"
[31:04] "How sweet is that? So, to be clear, man, the silliest thing to do once again is just to let like the fluctuations of Q4 just fucking smack you on the chin. You know, like there's nothing there's nothing that you know is beneficial for you when you let that happen. You you once again, you offload those costs onto the consumer."
[31:24] "That's also a good signal that it's time to raise the price. Okay."
[31:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Andrew's Ceiling Problem Solution"
NÚMEROS: $5K → $7,800 (sweet spot), $9K = 50% close rate drop, $1,200 gap to fill
VISUAL: Problema de value ceiling encontrado, solução através de produtos suplementares vs aumento direto
[31:30] "Now, to be fair, what do you do in Andrew's situation where he's kind of already found the ceiling? Remember what he said? He went from 5K to 7,800. Well, he tried 9K, but 9K dropped his closing in half."
[31:43] "So, to be clear and fair, he could articulate his value differently, and that could still enable him to go to 9K, but he kind of already found that ceiling. He's pretty close to it already."
[31:54] "So if he went from 7,800 to 9K throughout Q4 when his costs inflate, that could actually lower the overall quantity of revenue that comes into the business based on hitting that ceiling again, right? That comes with an entirely different set of challenges in itself."
[32:10] "What do you do in that situation? Anybody?"
[32:13] "You focus on the lifetime value of the consumer."
[32:16] "So to be clear, in his case, he could stick with the 7,800, but he needs to introduce something as like a supplement product. Okay, so watch this. The difference between the 7,800 and the 9K is only $1,200. What could he add as a supplement to get the lifetime value of the consumer up for $1,200 perceptually different?"
[32:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Steakhouse Upsell Strategy"
NÚMEROS: $80 steak + $19 butter = $99 total (not perceived as $99 steak), 24% increase in AOV
VISUAL: Exemplos de upsells: truffle butter ($19), bone marrow butter, lobster tail topping, separação perceptual de produtos
[32:40] "Because to be clear, I'll give you an example, right? I've mentioned steak a lot today. I guess I want a steak or something. There's a great business. It's a chain. Not sure if it's international or just here in the United States. You got Flemings and Mortons steakhouses. Okay. They upsell."
[32:57] "And there's there's another one auxiliary shout out Mastros. Okay. You go to a steakhouse. What's the one thing they try to upsell you the most when you order this steak besides?"
[33:08] "Yeah, that's one thing. What else for the steak specifically? Sanjay, what happens when you go to the steakhouse and they say, 'Would you like' Dude, Mastros has $19 butter that they will sell with the steak. $19."
[33:24] "for black truffle butter or for like bone marrow butter. They also do that stupid shit like the I've never ordered this to be clear, so excuse me if my terminology is off, but like you can like put like crab on it or like you can order like a lobster tail or something on the side or like some other shit you put on top of it."
[33:42] "You know what I'm talking about? It's like same thing. You're already buying a fucking $80 steak. You know, if they can get you to spend another $20 on butter, that's an insane difference percentage-wise for the average order value that they just got out of you."
[33:57] "But it's perceptually different. You don't look at it like you bought a $100 steak. You look at it like you bought a $80 steak with $19 butter. Does that make sense? It's a supplementary product."
[34:09] "So, you can also get the pricing up by adding something else that's perceptually separate. You see what I'm saying?"
[34:16] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Supplement Product Examples"
NÚMEROS: Mason's $10K + $1K/month community upsell, consulting + additional call time
VISUAL: Exemplos de produtos suplementares: comunidade pós-programa, call time adicional, perceptualmente separados do core offer
[34:18] "So, like what Mason said at the beginning of the call, he said, 'Look, I got this 10K thing at six months, then I upsell the community after for $1,000 a month.' You see, it's a supplement. Okay?"
[34:29] "We do consulting deals in my business. It's like when the client needs some additional talk time, we can upsell additional talk time, you know? We can upsell it like buy the call. Then it's like, well, you got your core rate, but then you got this like added on rate in addition to that, you know, perceptually different, separate."
[34:47] "See what I'm saying? So sometimes, to be clear, like once again, in Andrew's case, he already found the ceiling. $7,800 keeps his statistics perfect, but he might need to get that up a bit. He might need to get that up a bit from either Q4 inflation or just cuz he wants to get it up."
[35:04] "Whatever the reason is, supplement would be his addition that would make a lot more sense. What's something he could add? Even it doesn't have to be 1,200 bucks. be whatever the hell he wants it to be. Something that's just a easy yes. Something that's like a lay down deal."
[35:20] "Something that he could add to the offer, but instead he separates and has a has a specific price on to it. You get what I'm saying?"
[35:28] "All right, cool. Anybody have questions on any of this? Feel free to unmute yourself and ask away. Drop it in the chat if you want."
[35:36] "Um, I have one question, Jeremy. It's about saturated markets. Like, at the moment, I just use my offer as an example. I feel like I'm in a very saturated market, but I'm not giving up and I'm implementing all the strategies around content cycle bins to my own ads."
[35:54] "However, I do get a lot of resistance on price because it's viewed as so commoditized in my industry, even when I try to sort of pitch a differential value. And I just wondered what you think about pricing in saturated markets in general when you've had clients in saturated markets."
[36:10] "How close are you to everybody else?"
[36:13] "Like a bit more expensive, maybe 10% 15% more expensive than the commoditized version."
[36:19] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Commoditization Problem"
NÚMEROS: 10-15% price difference = still in same category
VISUAL: Conceito de separação categórica, necessidade de diferencial substancial para criar nova categoria mental
[36:21] "Yeah. So, how easy is it to compare you to the commoditized version when you're only 10 to 15% more?"
[36:28] "Like you're not in a separate category when you charge so close to it."
[36:32] "Yes. To be clear, you have to create additional separation with the pricing. Like I used in my agency offer as an example. Well, we do we do 15k and 10%. Is that comparable to the average commoditized agency?"
[36:47] "No."
[36:48] "Right. That separation to be clear once again like it puts it humans compartmentalize things. So, if you're if you're too close to what I've already compartmentalized things into, like if you're only 10 to 15% off in that example, it's easier to just put you into that existing bucket I've got."
[37:07] "Like I said, when you when you separate yourself through the signaling that your pricing gives off, they can't fit you in the same box. Something's got to be different when you're charging such a substantial difference. Does that make sense? And generally there is, to be clear, it does. It does."
[37:24] "Okay. Okay. What else? Anybody else got questions?"
[37:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Ideal Close Rate for Coaching"
NÚMEROS: 20-50% close rate = healthy, 80% = too low pricing
VISUAL: Matemática de dobrar preço: 80% → 40% close rate = mesmo revenue, probabilidade real 80% → 50-60%
[37:30] "I know you mentioned that the like 80% or whatever close rate was like too like is not something to really brag about. What would you say is like a a good close rate for like a coaching program?"
[37:42] "Depends on who's closing it. I I would say arguably as good as like 20 to 50% if it's a closer. You know, 80% just signals that you're charging far too little and that without exaggeration, you could likely double your price."
[37:58] "Because to be clear, like look at it for what it is. If I was closing 80% and I doubled my price and my closing rate went to 40%, I'd make the same amount of money. But what's the probability that I go from 80% to 40% on my close rate by doubling price?"
[38:15] "Low. I think there's a higher probability that I go from 80% to like 50 or 60%, therefore making more money by doubling the price in that example."
[38:25] "And and once again, I'd get a higher quality person coming through that specific offer that has a higher probability to get results. The thing is is like once again people who typically buy things for less are usually the people who have the lower probability to generate results"
[38:40] "whereas the people spending more especially on like a coaching offer like what you're saying typically have a higher probability to generate results."
[38:48] "Okay, cool. Thanks."
[38:50] "That make sense?"
[38:51] "Yeah."
[38:52] "Okay. Other questions?"

[38:56] "Yeah. Hell yeah. No. And you work with half the people."

[48:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Quality of Information Trumps Everything"
NÚMEROS: 1,000-3,000 YouTube views when starting, Mark: $2M/month with 12K followers
VISUAL: Demonstração de que qualidade de informação supera tamanho de audiência, conceito de "hidden gem"
[48:44] "That's one part of it for sure. The other main thing, so like when you're smaller and you're like, you know, in that position, the biggest thing is quality of information. So, like as an example, when I first started, I was a nobody. But like, dude, my quality of information was fucking off the chain, dude."
[49:02] "There not a single person talking about what I was talking about because they were my unique thoughts, content ad strategies. And my first strategy I came up with, unique to me, the Venus fly trend, content sequence followed by direct response. Not a soul talking about that made perfect sense as a strategy. [49:22] "So, it's like even before I had the results, the strategy made sense. The quality of information was there. My YouTube videos get like a,000 to 3,000 views. The viewership's not there, but dude, the quality of information, dog, if I wasn't me and I found my YouTube channel, I'd be fucking amped."
[49:41] "Like, my dude, the video I have dropping today is fucking insane. Like, I watch my own videos sometimes and I'm like, damn. You know, that's a banger. You know what I mean? Because just quality of information, you know what I mean? So, it's like that's the main thing. When you're not huge, it literally just comes down to quality of information, how you articulate it, you know? Does that make sense?"
[50:04] "Yeah, that makes sense."
[50:06] "I know no I know total nobody's zero presence that have insane quality of information that make like hundreds of thousands of dollars a month that have like 2,000 followers. I had a guy, he's actually in the inner circle, his name's Mark. He's speaking at our Q1 mastermind."
[50:23] "To be clear, Mark got his business up to $2 million a month. He had 12,000 followers at the time. He'd get less than like a couple hundred views max on his average videos, his $7,500 offer."
[50:37] "But bro, his quality of information off the fucking charts, dude. Like you'd watch this guy and you'd just be like, 'Holy shit.' You know? Like that was so good. You know, it felt like you found a true a true hidden gem. You know what I mean?"
[50:52] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Hole-in-the-Wall Restaurant Analogy"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Analogia de restaurante escondido com comida excepcional, descoberta do "chosen one", qualidade supera marketing
[50:54] "You ever go to like a hole-in-the-wall fucking restaurant and you're just like you're like the only person there. They bring out that food, you take a bite and you're like, 'Holy shit.' You know, I found the chosen one here. Oh my god."
[51:07] "You know, it's like that's that's how that's how it is when you're an unknown personal brand. You don't need like a big brand. It's all about quality of information. That's the biggest thing that builds your brand when you're first starting and you're a nobody. That make sense?"
[51:22] "Like before everything else comes, that's the egg that comes before the chicken in that example. Does that make sense?"
[51:28] "Yeah. No, for sure."
[51:30] "Okay, great. All right, sweet. Anybody else got questions, comments, anything you want to contribute?"
[51:37] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Q4 Mastermind Announcement"
NÚMEROS: October 19-20, Saturday = main day, last event at farm mansion
VISUAL: Detalhes do evento: speakers no sábado, networking extensivo, hot seats no domingo, saída gradual domingo
[51:39] "Sweet. All right, listen. Q4 mastermind's coming up. House is going to be fucking packed, okay? Looking forward to seeing everybody. It's October 19th and the 20th. Let me just set your expectations since there's a bunch of new people here."
[51:54] "The 19th specifically, Saturday, that's the day that you want to make sure that you're there. That's the day we do all our speakers. That's the day we have the longest networking time. That's the day where it's most active."
[52:06] "Sunday, people start peeling out throughout the day to go fly home, so they're back to work by Monday. Sunday is not as banging as Saturday, but regardless, you know, Sunday's great, too, for a networking day. We do the hot seat exercise on Sunday. It's a fun day."
[52:22] "But regardless, Saturday the 19th, that's the day you want to make sure that you're there. And uh, you know, once again, it's going to be really exciting. This will be the last one at the farm mansion. So, uh, we always go out with a banger for that. And we got some great vendors coming to support us throughout that process. It's going to be a good time."
[52:42] "Looking forward to seeing everybody. And of course, you got questions, you hit me up and you let me know. Happy to happy to get everything answered. Um, the pinned details, the details of the mastermind are at the top of our group chat."
[52:54] "You just go to the group chat, you look at the bar at the top, says Q4 mastermind details, a little circle. You can click that, see all the speakers, see all the details, address, food, all that fun stuff, parking instructions, and uh, yeah, look forward to seeing everybody. All right, talk soon."
[53:12] "Thank you. Later. Thanks, Jeremy."
📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
$10K coaching program → dobra revenue de $10K/mês para $20K/mês em 2-3 meses
Resultado total em 6 meses: $60K adicional (ROI de 6x)
Proposta de aumento: $10K → $12.5K (apenas 25% quando deveria ser maior)
Andrew Inner Circle: $25K em um dia após 3 semanas (1 pagamento)
16 pessoas entraram no Inner Circle em setembro (sinal de preço baixo)
80% close rate = preço criminalmente baixo
20-50% close rate = saudável para coaching
Agência: $15K/mês + 10% net rev share (goal: $100K wire/mês = cliente net $1M)
A5 Kobe beef: $120/onça vs preço de supermercado
Hummus guy: $230 compra sem perguntar preço
COVID shipping containers: 4x aumento de custo
Q4 Election: $25 → $60 por lead qualificado (2.5x)
Andrew: $5K → $7.8K (sweet spot), $9K = 50% drop, $8.8K = sem mudança
Brandon: $9.97 → $12.97 (30% aumento), $350K no mês do relaunch
Ed Tax Strategist: $12.5K para economizar $300K-$400K/ano (proposta: 12.5K + 10% da economia)
Mark: $2M/mês com apenas 12K followers, menos de 200 views/vídeo, oferta $7.5K
Master Internet Marketing: 7 semanas, aulas 3-5h, uma de 6h, 100% activation rate
Q4 Mastermind: Outubro 19-20, último evento na farm mansion
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
6 Sinais para Aumentar Preço:
Getting Fucked Test: Valor entregue vs preço cobrado (ROI de 6x indica subpreço)
Price Signaling: O que seu preço comunica sobre qualidade (A5 Kobe vs Walmart)
Incentive Structure: Alinhamento de incentivos (rev share vs flat fee)
Friction Test: Zero resistência = preço muito baixo
Value Ceiling: Quando fricção aparece, articular melhor ou ajustar oferta
Alignment: Todos os fatores acima alinhados = "this is my guy"
Teste de Aumento:
Se zero fricção: DOBRAR preço imediatamente
Testar com poucas calls, não mês inteiro
Reversível: pode voltar ao preço antigo
Pior cenário: encontra o value ceiling (coisa boa)
Comunicação de Aumento:
Anúncio claro via text, call, email, stories
Timeline definido (não imediato se há pipeline)
Vai "limpar o pipeline" (anchor bias)
Spike de vendas no curto prazo
Pipeline fresco sem anchor bias depois
Preço Dinâmico Q4:
Ajustar diariamente/semanalmente baseado em CPA
Repassar inflação de custos para cliente
Não absorver diferenças de custo operacional
Especialmente relevante em ciclos eleitorais
Solução para Value Ceiling:
Produtos suplementares (perceptualmente separados)
Exemplo: $80 steak + $19 butter ≠ $99 steak
Mason: $10K programa + $1K/mês community
Consulting: core rate + adicional call time
Diferença de $1.2K pode vir de supplement, não aumento direto
Commoditização:
10-15% diferença = ainda mesma categoria mental
Precisa separação substancial (ex: $15K + 10% vs $5K flat)
Criar categoria mental diferente através do preço
ESTRUTURA:
Hierarquia de Importância em Personal Brand (Educação):
Qualidade de Informação (egg before chicken)
Marketing Moderno (não green screen e terno)
Vida Emulável (não Lamborghini alugada)
Capacidade de Gerar Resultados
Estrutura de Oferta Diferenciada
Taxa de Ativação Alta
Diferenciação de Oferta (Master Internet Marketing):
7 semanas de aulas ao vivo/gravadas
3-5h por aula (uma de 6h)
Homework libraries entre aulas
Worksheets durante aulas
Exame final
Acesso vitalício a futuras iterações
100% activation rate
Close Rate Saudável:
20-50% = pricing correto
80%+ = criminalmente subprecificado
Matemática: 80% → 40% com 2x preço = mesmo revenue
Realidade: 80% → 50-60% com 2x preço = mais revenue
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
ROI Examples:
Coaching: $10K → gera $60K em 6 meses (6x)
Inner Circle: ROI em 3 semanas (Andrew: $25K em 1 dia)
Inner Circle: ROI em 5 dias (Swan)
Tax Strategist: $12.5K → economiza $300K-$400K/ano (24x-32x)
Estruturas de Preço:
Agência Premium: $15K/mês + 10% net rev share (goal: $100K wire)
Agência Commoditizada: $5K/mês + 0% rev share
Coaching: $10K (6 meses) ou $2.5K/mês x 5
Produtos: $9.97 → $12.97 (30% aumento sem impacto)
Inner Circle: $2K → $3K → $4K progressão
Custos Operacionais Q4:
Leads qualificados: $25 → $60 (140% aumento)
Shipping containers COVID: 4x aumento
Repassar 100% ao cliente, não absorver
Payment Plans:
50/50 split pay in full vs payment plan
Pay in full: melhor prioridade, geralmente mesmos resultados
Payment plan: $2.5K/mês x 5 meses
Supplements/Upsells:
Steakhouse butter: +$19 em $80 steak (24% AOV increase)
Community: $1K/mês após programa $10K
Call time adicional: buy per call
Perceptual separation = easier yes
DURAÇÃO: 53 minutos FORMATO: Weekly group call / Coaching session ao vivo
